we as Christians live and move and have our being.<|6.72|><|endoftext|>

<|7.86|> There is no app, nor will there ever be, on our smartphone for how to go through time with Christ.<|16.08|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.52|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I want to share with you all a final story that I came across this week.<|5.60|>
<|7.28|> The great statesman Winston Churchill planned every detail of his funeral service.<|14.86|>
<|15.84|> He and the cathedral staff of St. Paul's in London called it Operation Hope Not,<|22.64|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And perhaps that's what Ian should call his meetings with some of you as you pre-plan your own funerals.<|6.36|>
<|8.08|> At the end of the service, the Archbishop of Canterbury blessed those gathered and all sang the national anthem.<|17.28|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> them.<|1.02|><|endoftext|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Before the withdrawal of the coffin, a bugler at the west end of the cathedral played the last post, the English version of our military's taps.<|14.70|>
<|16.26|> There followed a full minute of silence.<|20.62|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.36|> The silence was finally broken by another bugler at the east end of the cathedral, playing reveille.<|9.68|>
<|11.28|> My friends, it is in that silence, in that time and space between endings and beginnings, beginnings and endings,<|24.62|>
<|24.62|> that it is in that silence.<|25.40|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> that we as Christians live and move and have our being.<|6.72|><|endoftext|>
